title: Tabbouleh
subtitle: Levantine herb salad with bulgur, tomato and lemon.
excerpt: A handful of bulgur wheat to a mountain of flat-leaf parsley, finely chopped with mint, spring onion, tomato, lemon juice and olive oil. The grain is a seasoning here, not a base. Ready in 30 minutes, better after an hour in the fridge. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Classical Lebanese-Palestinian tabbouleh. The parsley-dominant ratio is the traditional Levantine measure, as opposed to the bulgur-heavy Western adaptations. Claudia Roden's A Book of Middle Eastern Food (1968) and later New Book of Middle Eastern Food (2000) are the foundational English-language sources for the correct herb-to-grain proportion.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. Best eaten within 24 hours; parsley wilts and loses its bite beyond that.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The bulgur can be soaked and the vegetables chopped a few hours ahead; toss with the herbs and dressing just before serving.

content.text: The mistake most people make with tabbouleh is treating it as a bulgur salad with herbs. It is a parsley salad with a little bulgur. The ratio is stark: two large bunches of flat-leaf parsley to a few tablespoons of grain. The parsley is chopped very fine. The spring onions, tomatoes and mint are there for support. The lemon and olive oil are generous.

No cooking. The bulgur soaks in cold water or in the juice of the tomatoes; it needs 20 to 30 minutes to soften. While it soaks, chop the parsley: a rocking cut, the knife rhythmic and close, until the leaves are fine but not a paste. The size matters; coarse parsley makes the salad rough and heavy.

items.prepNote: leaves and tender stalks, very finely chopped — about 2 large bunches

content.text: Put {{bulgur-wheat}} of bulgur wheat into a small bowl. Pour over just enough cold water to cover by 1 cm. Leave for 20 to 30 minutes until the grain has absorbed the water and swollen. Drain off any excess. Spread on a clean tea towel and press dry.

Pile {{parsley-flat}} of flat-leaf parsley on the board. Rock the knife back and forth until the leaves are very finely chopped, not bruised. Repeat with {{mint}} of mint leaves. The difference between coarse and fine here is noticeable in the finished salad.

Tip the drained bulgur, parsley, mint, {{spring-onion}} sliced spring onions, and {{tomato}} of diced tomatoes into a large bowl. Squeeze over {{lemon}} lemon juice, pour over {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil and add {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Toss well. Taste and adjust lemon and salt; the salad should be sharp and bright.

Leave in the fridge for 30 minutes before serving. The herbs will absorb the dressing and the tomato will release a little juice that seasons the grain further. Serve at room temperature or lightly chilled.

items.cause: Tomatoes not deseeded, or bulgur not drained properly.
items.fix: Deseed the tomatoes and press the drained bulgur dry on a tea towel before adding.

items.cause: Water too cold, or grain not left long enough.
items.fix: Switch to warm water; 40 minutes should be enough. Fine-grade bulgur softens faster than coarse.

items.cause: Not enough lemon juice, or parsley chopped too coarsely.
items.fix: Add more lemon juice in small increments. Rethink the chop on the herbs next time — finer gives more surface area and brighter flavour.

items.symptom: Parsley turned dark and mushy overnight
items.cause: Dressed too far ahead or stored in too warm a spot.
items.fix: Dress within 30 minutes of serving. Leftovers keep one day maximum in the fridge.

content.text: Tabbouleh is the everyday salad of Lebanon, Syria and Palestine, served as part of the meze table alongside hummus, flatbread and pickles. In the Levant it appears at almost every meal; the version that travelled west and became a supermarket staple bears little resemblance to the original, which is almost entirely herb. It belongs in summer wherever there are ripe tomatoes and a moment's patience with a knife.
